titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Duffin to Celia Duffin, 9 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Maria Duffin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter is part of a rich correspondence between various members of the Duffin family, a large prosperous family living in Strandtown, Belfast. Several family members served in the war, including Major Terence Duffin, who served as a staff officer with 107 Brigade, and later with Royal Irish Rifles; Major Charles G Duffin MC, Royal Field Artillery; and their sisters Emma, Celia and Dorothy who served as Voluntary Aid Detachments (VADS) in Egypt and France, and with the YMCA.
This letters, from Maria to her daughter Celia, is filled with news of family and friends. She mentions that Terry has arrived. They have visited Wolfhill. She also writes that Aunt Elizabeth is dying.
She writes they met Mrs Lee Dawne after her return from Siberia. She notes that Mrs Dawne is very thin as she had to march 20 miles a day, suffered cold and hunger, as well as the hardships of a retreating army, including witnessing dead men along the route who had perished from hunger and cold

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
D2109/10/1/A Jan. 9: 1916 DUNOWEN,
BELFAST. Dearest Celia. Terry arrived this
morning, looking very well.
He wired & telephoned to you
to meet him for lunch. but
had no reply. I suppose you
had gone to Boad. He will be in London on
next Friday again: perhaps
you could arrange to meet
someway. It is a joy to
have him for a few days. We were at Wolfhill yester—
day: we went for a walk,
but the usual rain came
on. They are feeling very
dreary. Naturally and I
am sorry for both. Old Aunt Elizabeth is dying,
I had post a card from
Mrs Morris and she gave no
particulars, but said she
was unconscious and slept
most of the time. Under the
circumstances, I saw no good
in going. but told her to
inform Aunt E's . if
she was in correspondence
with them. I have heard no
more and suppose she may
linger for a day or two, poor
old lady! We were at a musical at home
at Mrs on Thursday:
the usual people. Mrs
P. Cott. Higgins had a solemn
colloquy with me about
Dorothy. He was afraid we
might think they had spread
the news of her engagement.
Mr Horburgh had spoken to
Mrs Cadell, so it was all out
and I had to make the best
of it and say there never
had been a real engagement.
but it is very awkward.
J. has a very stiff neck.
got. I suppose. by coming
out of very hot rooms at
Mrs B's and going on with
me to the adults tea party
at the However, it
is better today. No letter
yet from Emma. It is a
fortnight since we had
one. but Mrs Finlay had
heard and the Floyd Campbells
had an acknowledgment of
some sweets, so I suppose she
is all right. We met Mrs Lee Dawne after
her Serbian adventures. She
is very thin and had to
march 20 miles a day and
suffered cold & hunger. and
all the hardships of a retreating
army. but is very cheery
about it all. Dead men &
horses all along the route.
who perished from hunger
& cold. I am glad Dorothy
was not with her. Mrs
Calwell and others are
Aurthian employed.
D2109/10/1/A 2 DUNOWEN, BELFAST. as nurses. I have written to engage
a young cook who turned
up and hope to get her
settled in soon, but she is
reported noisy. and has not
been in good places. They
are very scarce, and I want
a cheap one. there times.
I suppose you are just
plodding along, I fear having
a dull monotonous time,
Dorothy is swithering as to what
to do. Colonel Damet has
just informed her that no
V.A.Ds get paid anything
until after three years full
training! Such is
the knowledge he possesses!
and he says he has V.A.Ds
in Hospitals under him,
which. I presume. are military! Ruth went back last Tuesday.
and comes over today for tea.
after lunching at Hopefield.
She thought Effe very
normal. When she saw her.
but was convinced she needed
a change. She was. evidently.
badly managed in the first
nursing Have and suffered
in consequence. I hope you got Emma's present
all right. Goodbye now darling. much
love. Is there no chance of
your getting any leave?
It seems hard after seven
long months. Your loving Mammy.
